## 1. Introduction

### 1.1 Purpose of this Document

This Software Requirements Specification describes what the JC Electrofields System (the "JCE System") is intended to do. It serves as the shared reference point between JC Electrofields and the development team, so that everyone agrees on what is being built before development begins.

Because the project is being scoped one department at a time, this document is deliberately a living draft. Several departments are now captured in detail — HR, Purchasing, Project Management, and the Warehouse — while Accounting and Business Development (BDD) are in active scoping, and Engineering and the public website remain structured placeholders awaiting their interviews. Each placeholder mirrors the scoped sections so that a future interview can be slotted into a consistent, pre-defined template.

### 1.2 Intended Audience

This document is written primarily for non-technical readers:

- **The Company Owner**, who needs to understand the full scope of the system, confirm that it matches the business intent, and approve the direction.
- **Department Stakeholders** (department heads and staff), who need to confirm that their department's requirements are captured correctly and to recognize which parts of their section still need their input.

The document is also written to be precise enough that the **development team** can use the completed sections — currently the HR module — as the basis for designing the underlying data structure and screen layouts.

### 1.3 Scope of the System

The JCE System has two major components that together support how JC Electrofields presents itself externally and operates internally:

1. **A public-facing company website.** An improved version of the company's web presence, with a focus on Search Engine Optimization (SEO) and Generative Engine Optimization (GEO) so the company is easier to find through both traditional search engines and AI-driven answer tools.

2. **An internal dashboard system.** A secure, role-based application used by staff across seven departments — HR, Accounting, Project Management, Purchasing, Engineering, Business Development (BDD), and the Warehouse. Access is controlled so that each person sees and can act only on the parts of the system relevant to their role, while the company owner has full access to everything.

This document covers both components. The internal dashboard's **six department modules are scoped and finalized** (HR, Accounting, Project Management, Purchasing, BDD, Warehouse), and the **public website is scoped** (§11). The **Engineering** module remains a structured placeholder awaiting its interview.

### 1.4 Definitions and Acronyms

| Term                                     | Meaning                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                        |
| ---------------------------------------- | ------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------ |
| **SRS**                                  | Software Requirements Specification — this document.                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                           |
| **JCE**                                  | JC Electrofields — the company for which the system is being built.                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                            |
| **Dashboard**                            | The internal, login-protected application used by staff to do their work.                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                      |
| **Module**                               | A self-contained area of the dashboard serving a specific department or function (e.g., the HR module).                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                        |
| **RBAC**                                 | Role-Based Access Control — controlling what each user can see and do based on their assigned role.                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                            |
| **Role**                                 | A named set of permissions assigned to a user (e.g., Owner, HR Head).                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                          |
| **CRUD**                                 | Create, Read, Update, Delete — the four basic operations performed on a record.                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                |
| **Record**                               | A single stored item, such as one employee's information.                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                      |
| **Owner**                                | The company owner, who has full access to every department and module.                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                         |
| **HR**                                   | Human Resources.                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                               |
| **OT**                                   | Overtime.                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                      |
| **Leave Request**                        | A formal request by an employee to be absent from work for a period of time.                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                   |
| **PIS**                                  | Personal Information Sheet — the company's existing form whose contents define the fields of an employee record.                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                               |
| **OB**                                   | Official Business — work conducted away from the usual workplace; captured by the Official Business / Travel form.                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                             |
| **LOA**                                  | Leave of Absence — an extended absence, filed either with pay or without pay.                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                  |
| **Leave Balance**                        | The amount of leave an employee has available; approved leave-type requests draw against it. _(Note: JCE does not track entitlements; recording-only.)_                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                        |
| **Sales Order No.**                      | The **system-wide primary key for projects** — the universal identifier (format YY-MM-XXX, assigned by BDD) that every module uses to reference a project. Stored on each Sales Order record (Section 9.3), referenced by Timekeeping (Working Project, Section 4.2), OB / Travel (Sales Order #, Section 4.3.1), and the future Accounting / Project Management / Purchasing / Engineering modules. Enforced globally unique. |
| **Manhours Distribution**                | The breakdown of a day's worked hours into pay-rate buckets (Regular Day, Rest Day, Special Holiday, Regular Holiday, and Absences/Undertime), each with its own sub-categories.                                                                                                                                                                                                                                               |
| **Regular Holiday**                      | A holiday officially declared as such (paid at the regular-holiday rate).                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                      |
| **Special Holiday**                      | A holiday officially declared as a special (non-working or working) day, paid at the special-holiday rate.                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                     |
| **Rest Day**                             | **Sunday for all employees, always.** Treated as a Day Type value in the dropdown (not a separate flag); work on a rest day is paid at the rest-day rate.                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                      |
| **Night Differential**                   | A pay premium for hours worked during the defined night-shift window.                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                          |
| **Day Type**                             | The classification of a given calendar date for pay purposes: Ordinary, Special Non-Working, Double Special Non-Working, Regular Holiday, or Double Regular Holiday. Determined from the holiday calendar.                                                                                                                                                                                                                     |
| **Double Special Non-Working Day**       | A calendar date on which two Special Non-Working Day declarations coincide; paid at a higher multiplier than a single Special Non-Working Day.                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                 |
| **Double Regular Holiday**               | A calendar date on which two Regular Holidays coincide; paid at a higher multiplier than a single Regular Holiday.                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                             |
| **DOLE Pay-Rate Matrix**                 | The Department of Labor and Employment table that maps each combination of Day Type, Rest Day overlap, Night Shift, and Overtime to a pay multiplier. Held as a configuration table in the system.                                                                                                                                                                                                                             |
| **Pay Multiplier**                       | The factor applied to an employee's base rate for a given "Work on" combination (e.g., 100%, 130%, 200%). Looked up from the DOLE Pay-Rate Matrix; not entered per row.                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                        |
| **Workflow**                             | A defined sequence of steps a request or task moves through (e.g., submitted → reviewed → approved).                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                           |
| **SEO**                                  | Search Engine Optimization — improving visibility in traditional search engines.                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                               |
| **GEO**                                  | Generative Engine Optimization — improving visibility and accurate representation within AI-generated answers.                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                 |

## 2. Overall Description

### 2.1 Product Perspective

The JCE System is a new, custom-built system made up of two related but distinct parts:

- The **public website** faces outward, toward customers, partners, and search tools. Its job is visibility, credibility, and information.
- The **internal dashboard** faces inward, toward employees. Its job is to run day-to-day departmental operations behind a secure login.

The two parts share the JC Electrofields identity and may share branding and some content, but they serve different audiences and have different access rules: the website is public, while the dashboard is private and permission-controlled. They are described together in this document because they form one overall system initiative, even though they will be scoped and may be delivered in stages.

Within the dashboard, each department is represented as its own module. The modules sit side by side under a single login and a shared navigation structure, and they are tied together by a common access-control layer (described in Section 3) that decides which modules and actions each user can reach.

### 2.2 User Classes

The system serves several classes of user. Their permissions are defined in Section 3; their general profiles are described here.

| User Class                                                    | Description                                                                                                              | Status                      |
| ------------------------------------------------------------- | ------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------ | --------------------------- |
| **Company Owner**                                             | Full access to every department, module, and action; oversees all departments.                                           | Confirmed                   |
| **Admin**                                                     | System configuration — users, roles, lookups, signatories, reference data; reads business records (distinct from Owner). | Confirmed                   |
| **Employee (Self-Service)**                                   | Submits own HR requests and views own payslip, leave, and timekeeping — own records only.                                | Confirmed (v1 self-service) |
| **HR Head / Timekeeper**                                      | HR module — employee records, timekeeping, and HR requests; the HR Head approves/records requests.                       | Confirmed (HR finalized)    |
| **Accounting (Staff · Lead/CFO · Payroll Officer)**           | Accounting module; compensation limited to Payroll Officer + Owner.                                                      | Confirmed (§5 finalized)    |
| **Project Management (PM Head · PMG Staff)**                  | Projects, BOQ, accomplishment/billing, and Material Requests.                                                            | Confirmed (scoped)          |
| **Purchasing (Staff · Supervisor · Finance/AP · Management)** | POs, RFPs, suppliers, import tracking, and approvals.                                                                    | Confirmed (scoped)          |
| **Warehouse (Warehouse · Site Engineer)**                     | Inventory master, stock movements, MRR/release/transfer; site engineers scoped to their sites.                           | Confirmed (scoped)          |
| **Business Development (Staff · Lead/Admin)**                 | Sales orders, offers, quotations, website content, and inquiries.                                                        | Confirmed (finalized)       |
| **Engineering**                                               | Engineering module roles.                                                                                                | Pending (placeholder)       |

> Detailed per-role permissions for every class are defined in the consolidated **RBAC matrix (Section 3)**. Only the **Engineering** roles remain pending, to be defined when that department is scoped.

### 2.3 Operating Environment

The internal dashboard is a secure, login-protected business application intended for use by JC Electrofields staff during their normal work. The public website is intended to be accessible to the general public through standard web browsers.

Specific environment details — such as where the system will be hosted, the range of devices it must support, and whether the dashboard must work on mobile as well as desktop — are to be confirmed with the company owner. No assumptions about these are made in this draft.

> **Status:** Operating-environment specifics (hosting, supported devices, offline needs, integrations with any existing tools) are **TBD** pending confirmation.

### 2.4 Design and Implementation Constraints

At this stage, the following constraints are known:

- The **timekeeping module must follow the company's existing format.** JC Electrofields already has a way of recording time, and the system must match that format rather than impose a new one. The exact format will be provided by the client.
- The system must enforce **role-based access control** across all departments (see Section 3), so that users only reach what their role allows.
- **Sales Order No. is the system-wide primary key for projects.** _Architectural decision._ Every module that references a project — Timekeeping, OB / Travel, the future Accounting, Project Management, Purchasing, and Engineering modules — does so via the Sales Order No. (format YY-MM-XXX, assigned by BDD; defined in Section 9.3). This identifier is enforced globally unique. It plays the same role for projects that BIO Nos plays for employees in the Timekeeping module — one identifier the whole system agrees on, owned by the department that creates it.

No technology choices — such as programming language, database engine, or framework — are specified in this document. Those decisions belong to the development team and will be recorded separately once made.

> **Status:** Additional constraints (e.g., data-retention rules, compliance requirements, branding guidelines, required integrations) are **TBD** and will be added as they are identified.

### 2.5 Assumptions & Dependencies

- **Single company, Philippine context.** The system serves JC Electrofields as a single entity; all amounts are in **PHP**, and statutory behavior follows Philippine rules (BIR, DOLE, RA 10173, SSS / PhilHealth / Pag-IBIG).
- **Iterative scoping.** Departments are scoped one at a time. As of this version, HR, Project Management, Purchasing, and Warehouse are scoped; Accounting and BDD have their decisions resolved but await JCE source documents; **Engineering (§8) and the Public Website (§11) remain structured placeholders** pending interviews.
- **Offline approvals.** Sign-offs are wet-signed on printed documents; the system tracks status only (no in-system e-signing) across HR requests, POs, RFPs, vouchers, MRRs, releases, and accomplishment reports.
- **Shared keys.** The **Sales Order No.** (BDD-owned), the **Project** entity (PMG-owned unifying parent over customer projects and internal cost centres), and the **MR No.** (PMG-owned) are first-class, system-wide identifiers other modules reference as foreign keys.
- **Full-history migration.** All historical records migrate at go-live (per the finalization decision), which implies a data-cleansing and validation effort before cutover.
- **Externalized configuration.** Rates, contribution tables, lookups, signatories, and approval thresholds are admin-editable and **versioned by effectivity date**; no business values are hard-coded.
- **Client-provided inputs.** Certain items can only be closed with JCE source documents (e.g., the §5.12 _client-input required_ list, the §9.8 BDD artifacts, and the Engineering/Website interviews). These are tracked per section.
- **Hosting & connectivity.** Reliable internet, a hosting environment, and modern-browser access on the staff's devices are assumed; the specific deployment/hosting arrangement is outside this SRS.

---

## 3. Access Control (RBAC) — Cross-Cutting

Access control applies across the entire dashboard, so it is described once here rather than repeated in every module. Every department module is expected to respect the rules defined in this section.

### 3.1 Access Control Model

The dashboard uses **Role-Based Access Control (RBAC)**. Instead of granting permissions to each person individually, the system defines **roles**, attaches a set of permissions to each role, and assigns roles to users. This keeps access consistent and easy to manage as staff join, change positions, or leave.

The model has three layers:

1. **The Owner role** sits above all departments and has **full access** — it can view, create, edit, and remove records in every module, and can oversee every department without restriction.
2. **Department-level roles** are scoped to a single department's module. A person with a department role can work within that department's module but, by default, cannot see or act in other departments' modules.
3. **Per-module permissions** determine, within a module a user can reach, exactly which actions they may take (for example, whether they can only view records, or also create, edit, and remove them).

### 3.2 Permission Scoping

Permissions are expected to be defined along two dimensions:

- **Which modules** a role can access (for example, the HR module, the Accounting module, and so on).
- **Which actions** the role can perform within each accessible module (for example: view only; view and create; view, create, and edit; or full control including removal).

This means the same person can have different levels of access in different modules if the business requires it, and the company owner can always reach everything.

### 3.3 Roles (consolidated, v1.62)

The role set below consolidates every department's role inputs into the system-wide roster. **One role per user** (a person needing more than one department's access is given the broader-fitting role or, where unavoidable, a separate account). Roles fall into three groups: **cross-cutting** (Owner, Admin, Employee self-service), **department** roles, and the **approval/oversight** roles that carry sign-off authority.

| Role                        | Home             | Summary                                                                                                                                                   |
| --------------------------- | ---------------- | --------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| **Owner**                   | System           | Full access to every module and action; ultimate oversight.                                                                                               |
| **Admin**                   | System           | Technical configuration — users, roles, lookups, signatories, reference/config data — across modules; read-only on business records. Distinct from Owner. |
| **Employee (Self-Service)** | Self             | Own records only: submit own HR requests; view own payslip, leave balance, and timekeeping; edit limited personal info.                                   |
| **HR Head**                 | HR               | Manage employee records and timekeeping; **approve/record** HR requests (the HR approver named on the forms). Compensation fields are masked (see §3.7).  |
| **Timekeeper**              | HR               | Create/edit timekeeping entries; verifies pay-period batches; reads the Sales Order / project list to pick the Working Project.                           |
| **Accounting Staff**        | Accounting       | Data entry across Sales, Collections, Vouchers, JV; no posting/approval; no compensation.                                                                 |
| **Accounting Lead / CFO**   | Accounting       | Approve, post, and run reports across Accounting; no compensation (restricted to Payroll Officer + Owner).                                                |
| **Payroll Officer**         | Accounting       | Full access to the Payroll sub-tab including compensation; reads employee records for payroll.                                                            |
| **PM Head**                 | Project Mgmt     | Full PMG — projects, BOQ, variation orders, accomplishment periods, retention release; approval authority.                                                |
| **PMG Staff**               | Project Mgmt     | Create/edit Material Requests; read projects/portfolio.                                                                                                   |
| **Purchasing Staff**        | Purchasing       | Create/edit POs, RFPs, suppliers; cannot approve or release payment.                                                                                      |
| **Purchasing Supervisor**   | Purchasing       | Internal PO verification/approval; supplier data quality.                                                                                                 |
| **Finance / AP**            | Purchasing/Acctg | RFP / payment processing; cross-reads Accounting payable workflow.                                                                                        |
| **Management / President**  | Oversight        | Final PO approval gates (e.g., import stages 4 & 7); read-only oversight.                                                                                 |
| **Warehouse**               | Warehouse        | Inventory master, stock movements, MRR, releases, transfers; document lock authority.                                                                     |
| **Site Engineer**           | Warehouse        | Create/submit (Draft → For Checking) MRRs, Release Forms & off-BOQ rows at **assigned sites only**; **cannot Lock/Unlock** (§10.14.1, §10.12).            |
| **BDD Staff**               | BDD              | Sales orders, offers, quotations, website content, inquiries.                                                                                             |
| **BDD Lead / Admin**        | BDD              | Approve, access audit log, manage BDD configuration.                                                                                                      |
| **Engineering role(s)**     | Engineering      | _Pending — defined when §8 Engineering is scoped._                                                                                                        |

### 3.4 Action verbs

The permission matrix uses a **rich action set** so module-specific behaviors (accounting posting, approvals, the warehouse lock gate) are expressible:

**View** · **Create** · **Edit** · **Delete/Void** · **Approve** (sign-off authority) · **Post** (commit to the ledger) · **Lock** (finalize a document, e.g., the warehouse 3-state gate) · **Export** (print / PDF / data export).

**Edit authority and verb authority are independent axes.** A role's authority to create/edit a record's lines (the **E** grant) is separate from its authority to run sign-off verbs (**Approve / Post / Lock**): a role may edit without holding any verb, and verbs attach to the role's home-module **F** grant (or an explicit suffix such as **R+A**, read-plus-approve). The UI realizes these as two distinct gates — live inputs for edit authority, and conditionally-present verb buttons that are simply **absent** for roles lacking the verb (never disabled-by-role).

### 3.5 Role × module access matrix

Cell codes: **F** = Full (all actions applicable to that module) · **E** = Create/Edit (no delete/approve) · **R** = Read-only · **—** = No access. Suffixes: **+A** Approve · **+P** Post · **+L** Lock. An **R** in a module that is not the role's home is a _scoped cross-module read_ (see §3.6).

| Role                    | HR  | ACC   | PMG | PUR | WH  | BDD | ENG | WEB | CFG |
| ----------------------- | --- | ----- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
| Owner                   | F   | F     | F   | F   | F   | F   | F   | F   | F   |
| Admin                   | R   | R     | R   | R   | R   | R   | R   | R   | F   |
| Employee (Self-Service) | own | —     | —   | —   | —   | —   | —   | —   | —   |
| HR Head                 | F+A | —     | —   | —   | —   | —   | —   | —   | —   |
| Timekeeper              | E   | —     | R   | —   | —   | R   | —   | —   | —   |
| Accounting Staff        | —   | E     | —   | R   | —   | —   | —   | —   | —   |
| Accounting Lead / CFO   | —   | F+A+P | —   | R   | —   | —   | —   | —   | —   |
| Payroll Officer         | R   | E     | —   | —   | —   | —   | —   | —   | —   |
| PM Head                 | —   | —     | F+A | R   | R   | —   | —   | —   | —   |
| PMG Staff               | —   | —     | E   | —   | R   | —   | —   | —   | —   |
| Purchasing Staff        | —   | —     | R   | E   | R   | —   | —   | —   | —   |
| Purchasing Supervisor   | —   | —     | R   | F+A | R   | —   | —   | —   | —   |
| Finance / AP            | —   | R     | —   | E   | —   | —   | —   | —   | —   |
| Management / President  | —   | —     | —   | R+A | —   | —   | —   | —   | —   |
| Warehouse               | —   | —     | R   | R   | F+L | —   | —   | —   | —   |
| Site Engineer           | —   | —     | R   | —   | E   | —   | —   | —   | —   |
| BDD Staff               | —   | —     | —   | —   | —   | E   | —   | E   | —   |
| BDD Lead / Admin        | —   | —     | —   | —   | —   | F+A | —   | E   | —   |
| Engineering role(s)     | —   | —     | —   | —   | —   | —   | TBD | —   | —   |

_Columns: HR · ACC = Accounting · PMG = Project Management · PUR = Purchasing · WH = Warehouse · BDD · ENG = Engineering · WEB = Public Website · CFG = System Configuration._

> **Site Engineer × WH = E (v1.74).** The earlier `R` understated this role: §10.14.1 grants the Site Engineer **create/submit** of MRRs, Release Forms, and off-BOQ rows for assigned sites — an **E** grant. Lock/Unlock remain Warehouse Admin/Owner verbs (§10.12, FR-LCK-05), withheld through the **edit-vs-verb independent axes** of §3.4 (E without the Lock verb), not expressed as a flat read. The implemented UI build models exactly this (`siteeng` `wh: "E"`, with Lock/Unlock/Adjustment/Confirm/Promote verbs absent for the role).

### 3.6 Cross-module read grants

Departments are **siloed by default**; the following **explicit read-only grants** exist because the workflows require them (matching the cross-module hooks in each section):

- **Timekeeper / PMG Staff / Purchasing / Warehouse → Sales Orders & Projects** — to select/reference the Working Project and Project (BDD 9.3 / §6.3.1).
- **Accounting (Staff, Lead, Finance/AP) → Purchasing PO & RFP** — for the payable workflow (§7.7).
- **PM Head & PMG Staff → Warehouse stock** — Available Stock for MR verification (§10.7); **PM Head → Purchasing PO & shipment** for the delivery cross-check (§6.6.9).
- **Purchasing (Staff, Supervisor) → PMG Material Requests** — to receive For-Purchase hand-offs (§6.10); **→ Warehouse stock** for context.
- **Warehouse → Purchasing PO** (goods receipt / MRR three-way match, §10.6) and **→ PMG Material Requests** (verification).
- **Site Engineer → assigned-project PMG data** (scoped to their sites).

### 3.7 Special access rules

- **One role per user.** Effective permissions are exactly the assigned role's; there is no permission union from multiple roles.
- **Owner vs Admin.** The **Owner** has full _business_ access to all data; the **Admin** holds _technical_ configuration (users, roles, lookups, signatories, reference data) and reads business records but does not create/approve/post them.
- **Sensitive data — compensation.** Employee compensation (rates, allowances, payslip amounts, payroll runs) is visible only to the **Payroll Officer** and **Owner**. the HR Head manages employee records but compensation fields are **masked**; the Accounting Lead/CFO sees Accounting but not compensation.
- **Approvals & amount thresholds.** Approval is the **Approve** permission carried by lead/head/oversight roles (HR Head, PM Head, Purchasing Supervisor, Accounting Lead/CFO, BDD Lead, Management/President, Owner). **Amount-threshold routing** (configurable in Settings, per Purchasing §7.15.4) escalates higher-value transactions to higher approvers.
- **Employee self-service.** The Employee role is scoped strictly to the user's **own** records — submit own HR requests, view own payslip / leave balance / timekeeping, and edit limited personal info; no visibility into other employees or other modules.
- **Engineering roles** are defined when §8 Engineering is scoped; until then no Engineering role is provisioned beyond Owner/Admin oversight.

## 4. HR Department Module _(scoped — finalized v1.64)_

The HR module is the first part of the dashboard to be built. It has been scoped through stakeholder interviews and is described in detail below. The module is organized into three areas, which are expected to appear as three sections in the HR navigation: **Employees**, **Timekeeping**, and **HR Requests**.

### 4.1 Employee Management

**Purpose.** To maintain accurate records of the company's employees and give HR a reliable, searchable source of employee information.

**Confirmed scope.** Full **CRUD** capability over employee records — HR can **create**, **view (read)**, **update**, and **archive** employee records. The detailed fields of an employee record come from the company's existing **Personal Information Sheet (PIS)** (see "Source documents" below).

**Expected behaviors.**

- **Add (Create):** HR can add a new employee by entering their information into a form and saving it as a new record.
- **View (Read) — grouped list view by Pay Period Type.** _Confirmed._ The default Employee Management screen presents employees in **three labeled sections stacked on one page**, in this order:
  - **Daily** — all employees whose Salary Rate Category is Daily.
  - **Weekly** — all employees whose Salary Rate Category is Weekly.
  - **Monthly** — all employees whose Salary Rate Category is Monthly.

  Each section is a labeled header (e.g., a row that says "Daily") with the matching employees listed underneath it. All three groups are visible on the same page; HR scrolls through to see everyone, but the section headers make it immediately clear which group each person belongs to without needing to read a column. Pay Period Type is derived from the Salary Rate Category on the employee record. From this view, HR can open any record to see its full details.

- **Search:** HR can quickly locate an employee — for example, by name or another identifying detail — without scrolling through the entire list. Search ignores the section grouping and shows matching employees regardless of which group they fall under (with the matching section indicated on each result).
- **Filter:** HR can narrow the employee list to a relevant subset (for example, by employment status or employment type). Filters apply within the grouped view (each section is filtered independently) and a section becomes hidden if it has no matches.
- **Edit (Update):** HR can change the information on an existing employee record and save the updates.
- **Archive (instead of delete):** _Confirmed._ Records are **archived, not permanently deleted.** Archiving removes an employee from active lists while preserving the full record and its history for future reference (for example, re-hires, audits, and historical reporting). A way to view and, if needed, restore archived records should be provided.

**Employment status lifecycle.** _Confirmed._ Each employee record carries a **status** that reflects where the employee is in their employment lifecycle. Status drives downstream behavior — for example, who appears in timekeeping and who is eligible to file requests. The exact set of statuses should be confirmed with HR; a typical set to validate is: _probationary, regular/active, on leave, suspended, resigned/terminated (archived)._

**Employment type and contracts.** _Confirmed._ Each employee record distinguishes **employment type** (for example, regular vs. contractual; full set to be confirmed from the PIS). For **contractual** employees, the record stores the **contract duration** (start and end dates), and the system **alerts HR when fewer than six (6) months remain** before the contract ends, so the contract can be renewed or closed out in time. _(To confirm: how the alert is delivered — e.g., an on-screen indicator and/or notification — and who receives it.)_

**Audit trail.** _Confirmed._ Changes to employee records are **tracked** — the system keeps a history of what changed, when, and by whom. This supports HR record-keeping and accountability.

**Employee record fields (from the Personal Information Sheet).** _Confirmed._ The employee record carries the following fields, grouped as the PIS organizes them. Items tagged _computed_ are calculated by the system rather than typed in. Items tagged _sensitive_ hold personal or financial information whose visibility should be restricted, even within HR (see Section 3, Access Control).

_Identification and assignment_

- **S/N** — Serial Number. _(To confirm: whether this is a true employee identifier or only a list index; if it is the identifier, whether it is auto-generated or entered.)_
- **Name of Employee.**
- **BIO Nos** — Biometric number(s). _Used by Timekeeping._ This is the link between an employee record and the biometric/clock data that drives the timekeeping module (Section 4.2).
- **Place of Assignment** — where the employee is currently assigned (site, project, or location). _(To confirm: whether this is a free-text field or selected from a list — for example, the list of projects from the future Project Management module.)_
- **Position.**
- **Date Hired.**
- **Years of Service** — _computed_ from Date Hired.
- **Status** — the employee's current employment status. Maps to the **employment status lifecycle** described above (e.g., probationary, regular/active, on leave, suspended, resigned/terminated). _(To confirm with HR: the exact set of allowed values.)_

_Compensation_ — _sensitive._ Visibility of this group should be restricted (see Section 3); it also feeds payroll in the future Accounting module.

- **Salary Rate Category** — also **drives the employee's pay-period schedule** (Monthly, Daily, or Weekly) used by Timekeeping for verification batching and hand-off to payroll. See Section 4.2 → _Pay periods, verification, and hand-off to payroll_. _(To confirm if a separate field is used.)_
- **Daily Rate (Basic).**
- **Equivalent Monthly Rate.**
- **Monthly Allowance.**
- **Duty Meal Allowance.**
- **Project Allowance.**
- **Total Monthly Compensation** — _computed_ (sum of monthly compensation components).
- **Daily (Basic + Allowances)** — _computed_ (daily rate including allowances).

_Government Mandatory Information_ — _sensitive._ These are statutory identification numbers and require careful handling under applicable data-privacy rules.

- **SSS Number** (Social Security System).
- **Pag-IBIG Number** (Home Development Mutual Fund).
- **PhilHealth Number** (Philippine Health Insurance Corporation).
- **TIN** (Taxpayer Identification Number).

_Personal Information_ — _sensitive._

- **Birthday.**
- **Age** — _computed_ from Birthday.
- **Gender.**
- **Address.**
- **Contact Number** (employee's own).

_Emergency Contact_

- **Contact Person In Case of Emergency.**
- **Contact Number** (of the emergency contact).

_Other_

- **Insurance.** _(To confirm: whether this is a yes/no indicator, the name of an insurance provider/policy, or a coverage detail.)_
- **Vaccinated.** _(To confirm: whether this is a yes/no indicator or a richer status — for example, vaccine type, number of doses, or booster status.)_
- **ATM Account Number** — _sensitive._ Used by payroll for salary disbursement.
- **Expiration Date.** _(To confirm: which item this date refers to — likely contract expiration for contractual employees, but could also be ID, ATM card, or another document. This drives whether it is the same date used by the sub-6-month contract-expiry alert above.)_
- **Remarks** — free-text notes.

**Cross-module touchpoints already visible in the record.**

- **BIO Nos → Timekeeping** (Section 4.2): the biometric number ties an employee to their recorded time.
- **Compensation block → Accounting / Payroll** (future module): salary, allowances, and ATM account drive payroll. The same record is the source of truth.
- **Place of Assignment → Project Management** (future module): if assignment is project-based, the list of valid values should come from that module once it is scoped.

**Statutory and data-privacy context.** The presence of SSS, Pag-IBIG, PhilHealth, and TIN places this system in the **Philippine** statutory context. That has two practical implications worth noting now: (a) payroll calculations in the future Accounting module will need to account for the corresponding mandatory contributions, and (b) the system holds personal data covered by the Data Privacy Act of 2012 (RA 10173), which reinforces the need for access restrictions on sensitive fields (compensation, government IDs, ATM, address) and a maintained audit trail. _(To confirm with the company owner: any specific compliance obligations beyond standard practice.)_

**Source documents.**

> **Status: PIS field list received and incorporated.** ✅ The field list above is taken from the company's Personal Information Sheet as provided. Remaining items are small clarifications listed inline (S/N as identifier, Status value set, Insurance and Vaccinated value types, Expiration Date meaning, Place of Assignment as free-text vs. list) and the **layout** of the PIS form (which is helpful for the add/edit screen design but not blocking).

**Notes for design.** This subsection is now enough to begin designing the employee data structure and the add/edit screens. The list view, detail view, and add/edit form can be built directly from the field list above, with computed fields generated by the system, sensitive groups (Compensation, Government IDs, ATM) gated by access rules, and the change history layered on top as described earlier.

### 4.2 Timekeeping

**Purpose.** To record employees' working time within the dashboard, digitizing the company's current spreadsheet-based process, and to produce finalized hours that feed payroll.

**Confirmed scope.** A timekeeping module that **mirrors the company's existing spreadsheet.** Today, time is tracked in a spreadsheet; the module must reproduce that same layout and feel so it is familiar to the people entering the data, rather than introducing a new structure.

**Confirmed behaviors.**

- **Weekly time entry:** _Confirmed._ Time is entered **per employee on a weekly basis (a seven-day week).** Each employee's week is recorded together, matching how the current spreadsheet is filled in.
- **Per-project time, with multi-project days:** _Confirmed._ Time is recorded **against the project it was worked on**, so labor cost can be attributed to the correct project. The **Working Project field sits beside Date** at the row level, and a **single date can have multiple rows — one per project**. **All project rows for the same date share the same Time In and Time Out** (the actual hours the employee was at work), and the day's **Manhours Distribution is divided evenly across the project rows** for cost allocation: two projects → each row gets half of every count; three projects → each row gets a third; and so on. This is essential for accurate project costing and is described in detail in "Multi-project days and cost allocation" below.
- **Finalize and hand off to Accounting:** _Confirmed._ Once a period's hours are finalized, the **finalized hours are passed to the Accounting department for payroll.** The timekeeping module is therefore the source of the hours used to pay employees.

**Cross-module dependencies (now known).**

- **Employee Record link:** Each time entry is tied to an employee via **Employee Number**. The employee's **Position** (and, by extension, Name) is taken from the employee record, so changes there propagate to timekeeping rather than being re-typed.
- **Projects / Costing:** Because time is recorded per project (the **Working Project** field), Timekeeping connects to the future **Project Management** module so that labor cost rolls up to the right project. The list of projects an employee can log against will ultimately come from that module.
- **Accounting / Payroll:** Finalized hours — broken out by the Manhours Distribution buckets — flow from Timekeeping into the future **Accounting** module to drive payroll, since each bucket is paid at a different rate. Even though Accounting is not yet scoped, this hand-off is a confirmed requirement and should shape how finalized data is structured.

**Timekeeping entry fields (from the spreadsheet).** _Confirmed._ The timesheet has two levels: a small **header** identifying whose timesheet it is (these fields don't change row-by-row), and the **per-row entries** where work is recorded. A row captures **one date, one project** — and because an employee can work on more than one project in a day, **a single date can have multiple rows** (one per project), so the day's hours can be allocated across projects.

Items tagged _auto-populated_ are filled in by the system from the employee record once the employee is selected; the user does not retype them.

_Header — per employee, per timesheet_

- **Employee Number** — links the timesheet to the employee record. _(To confirm: whether this is the **S/N** from the PIS or a separate employee-number field; either way, this is the canonical identifier used for time entry.)_
- **Employee Name** — _auto-populated_ from the employee record.
- **Position** — _auto-populated_ from the employee record.

_Per-row entry — one date, one project_

Each row carries the fields below. The timekeeper enters all of them; there is no per-row field that depends on an automated calendar lookup.

- **Date** — entered by the timekeeper.
- **Working Project** — sits **beside Date** on the row. The project the row's hours are attributed to. A timekeeper can record **multiple projects on the same date** by adding one row per project. **Sourced from the Sales Orders list (Section 9.3)** — specifically, Sales Orders whose Project Status is Ongoing Project, On Hold, or Project Completed, and that have not been Archived. The dropdown displays the Sales Order No. (e.g., `26-05-378`) plus a short label so the timekeeper can pick by either.
- **Day Type** — selected by the timekeeper from a **dropdown beside Date**. One of six values: Regular Day · **Rest Day (= Sunday)** · Special Holiday · Double Special Holiday · Regular Holiday · Double Regular Holiday. The timekeeper is the source of truth for whether a date is a holiday (and which kind), since the system does not maintain an automated holiday calendar.
- **Time In** — entered by the timekeeper.
- **Time Out** — entered by the timekeeper.
- **Manhours Distribution** — the row's hours, broken out into the Day Type bucket selected above, with the sub-categories listed below.
- **Remarks** — free-text notes for the row.

> **Why the dropdown.** Philippine holidays (Special and Regular) are declared annually by government proclamation, so the system cannot reliably auto-detect them without an external feed or a manually maintained calendar. Putting the Day Type in the timekeeper's hands keeps the system simple — no calendar to import, no calendar to maintain — and gives the timekeeper (who already knows the day's classification from payroll memos and DOLE announcements) the direct say.

_Manhours Distribution (per row)_ — the row's worked time is broken into the **Day Type bucket** the timekeeper picked, with sub-categories that capture how the hours within that bucket break down. Each row uses exactly **one** Day Type bucket. **The split is computed by the system from Day Type + Time In + Time Out** — the timekeeper does not enter the sub-category numbers manually. The calculation rules the system applies are specified in "Calculation rules — auto-computing the Manhours Distribution" below.

- **Regular Day** _(DOLE: Ordinary day)_
  - Regular Hours
  - Overtime
  - Night Differential
- **Rest Day** _(= Sunday, always)_
  - Regular Hours
  - Overtime Hours
  - Night Differential
- **Special Holiday** _(DOLE: Special Non-Working Day)_
  - Regular Hours
  - Overtime Hours
  - Night Differential
- **Double Special Holiday** _(DOLE: Double Special Non-Working Day)_
  - Regular Hours
  - Overtime Hours
  - Night Differential
- **Regular Holiday**
  - Regular Hours
  - Overtime Hours
  - Night Differential
- **Double Regular Holiday**
  - Regular Hours
  - Overtime Hours
  - Night Differential
- **Absences and Undertime**
  - Absences
  - Undertime

> **Structural note on Rest Day.** Rest Day is a **Day Type value, not a separate flag**, and is defined as **Sunday for all employees, always**. The timekeeper picks "Rest Day" from the dropdown for any Sunday on which an employee worked but for which no other classification applies. If a date is both a Sunday _and_ a declared holiday (e.g., Labor Day falling on a Sunday), the timekeeper picks the **holiday classification** (the higher pay category) — see the open question on compound cases in the Appendix.

**Calculation rules — auto-computing the Manhours Distribution.** The system fills in Regular Hours, Overtime, and Night Differential for the row's selected Day Type bucket from three inputs: the Day Type, Time In, and Time Out. The timekeeper does not type these numbers.

_Configuration constants_ (system-wide; can be tuned later if needed):

| Constant                  | Value                            |
| ------------------------- | -------------------------------- |
| Regular workday           | 8 hours                          |
| Lunch break               | 12:00 PM – 1:00 PM (60 minutes)  |
| OT meal break             | 10:00 PM – 11:00 PM (60 minutes) |
| Night meal break          | 2:00 AM – 3:00 AM (60 minutes)   |
| Night Differential window | 11:00 PM – 6:00 AM               |

_Algorithm._ For each row, the system computes:

1. **Elapsed time** = Time Out − Time In (handling shifts that cross midnight by adding 24 hours where needed).
2. **Break deductions**, applied when a break window is _fully spanned_ by the worked window (i.e., the worker was clocked in for the entire break):
   - Lunch break (12:00 PM – 1:00 PM): deduct 60 minutes if Time In ≤ 12:00 PM **and** Time Out ≥ 1:00 PM.
   - OT meal break (10:00 PM – 11:00 PM): deduct 60 minutes if the worked window fully covers 10:00 PM – 11:00 PM.
   - Night meal break (2:00 AM – 3:00 AM): deduct 60 minutes if the worked window fully covers 2:00 AM – 3:00 AM.
   - **Net worked** = elapsed − sum of applicable break deductions.
3. **Regular Hours** = min(net worked, 8).
4. **Overtime** = max(net worked − 8, 0).
5. **Night Differential** = portion of the worked window that falls inside the Night Differential window (11:00 PM – 6:00 AM), minus 60 minutes if the night meal break (2:00 AM – 3:00 AM) is fully spanned within that overlap.

The three numbers go into the picked Day Type bucket; all other Day Type buckets remain empty for that row. **Regular Hours and Overtime are mutually exclusive** (each hour of net worked time is one or the other). **Night Differential is a separate count** of hours in the night window — those same hours are _also_ recorded under Regular Hours or Overtime, depending on whether they fall before or after the 8-hour threshold. The totals can therefore exceed the net worked time (see the example below). This is intentional: payroll uses all three counts together to apply the right multiplier for each hour, including compound cases such as an hour that is both Overtime and inside the night window.

_Worked example_ — Maria works 8:00 AM to 2:00 AM (next day) on a Regular Day.

| Step                                     | Computation                                                  |
| ---------------------------------------- | ------------------------------------------------------------ |
| Elapsed time                             | 18 hours                                                     |
| Lunch break (12–1 PM) fully spanned?     | Yes (8 AM ≤ 12 PM, 2 AM ≥ 1 PM) → deduct 1 hour              |
| OT meal break (10–11 PM) fully spanned?  | Yes (8 AM ≤ 10 PM, 2 AM ≥ 11 PM) → deduct 1 hour             |
| Night meal break (2–3 AM) fully spanned? | No (shift ends at 2 AM, doesn't reach 3 AM) → no deduction   |
| Net worked                               | 16 hours                                                     |
| Regular Hours                            | min(16, 8) = **8**                                           |
| Overtime                                 | max(16 − 8, 0) = **8**                                       |
| Night Differential window overlap        | 11 PM – 2 AM = 3 hours; night meal break not spanned → **3** |

The row records, under the **Regular Day** bucket: Regular Hours **8**, Overtime **8**, Night Differential **3**. All other Day Type buckets stay empty. Note that the three counts add to 19, but only 16 hours were actually worked — the 3 Night Differential hours are the OT hours that fell after 11 PM, recorded a second time under Night Differential so payroll can identify them for the compound premium.

_Standard shifts at JCE._ Most employees work either **7:00 AM – 4:00 PM** or **8:00 AM – 5:00 PM**. Both span the lunch break, both yield 8 hours net worked, both result in Regular Hours **8** with no Overtime and no Night Differential. The algorithm handles them identically.

_Multi-project days and cost allocation._ When an employee works on **more than one project on the same date**, the row count multiplies, but the day's _total_ worked time and Manhours Distribution stay the same. The pattern is:

1. The employee has **one Time In and one Time Out** for the day (the actual hours they were at work).
2. The system **computes the Manhours Distribution once** for the day using the algorithm above.
3. The timekeeper enters **one row per project** worked that day, all sharing the same Date, Day Type, Time In, and Time Out.
4. The day's Regular / Overtime / Night Differential counts are **divided evenly across the project rows**. Two projects → each row gets half of every count. Three projects → each row gets a third. And so on. The total across rows always equals the day's computed distribution.
5. Each row carries its share into payroll's per-project costing — labor cost is attributed evenly to each project the employee worked on that day.

_Multi-project worked example._ Maria works 8:00 AM – 2:00 AM on a Regular Day, on two projects (Project Alpha and Project Beta). The day's distribution is computed once (the worked example above): Regular **8**, Overtime **8**, Night Differential **3**. The two project rows split those counts evenly:

| Row | Date       | Working Project | Day Type    | Time In | Time Out | Regular | OT  | Night Diff |
| --- | ---------- | --------------- | ----------- | ------- | -------- | ------- | --- | ---------- |
| 1   | 2026-05-25 | Project Alpha   | Regular Day | 8:00 AM | 2:00 AM  | 4       | 4   | 1.5        |
| 2   | 2026-05-25 | Project Beta    | Regular Day | 8:00 AM | 2:00 AM  | 4       | 4   | 1.5        |

Both rows sum back to the day's totals (Regular 8, OT 8, Night Diff 3). The cost flows half to each project. The Manhours Distribution counts can be fractional (e.g., 1.5 hours) when the day's totals don't divide evenly by N.

_(Future flexibility, if needed: the timekeeper could be allowed to override the even split — e.g., to log 6 hours on Project A and 2 hours on Project B for an 8-hour day — when the work was clearly weighted toward one project. The default is even N-way split, matching JCE's current practice.)_

**Mapping the DOLE pay-rate matrix ("Work on" → data model).** The Department of Labor and Employment (DOLE) pay-rate matrix lists every combination under which an hour can be worked — ordinary day, rest day, special holiday, regular holiday, the "double" variants, plus night-shift and overtime modifiers — and the corresponding pay multiplier (e.g., 100%, 130%, 200%, 260%, and so on). Built as a flat list, it has roughly forty rows. Asking the timekeeper to pick the right "Work on" row from such a list, or asking the payroll manager to apply the multipliers by hand, would be slow and error-prone.

The cleaner mapping is to recognize that the matrix is the **product of four orthogonal facts**, and to capture those facts on each timekeeping row rather than capturing the combinations:

| Dimension               | Possible values (as used in the system)                                                                                 | How the system knows it                                                                                                    |
| ----------------------- | ----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- | -------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| **Day Type**            | Regular Day · Rest Day (= Sunday) · Special Holiday · Double Special Holiday · Regular Holiday · Double Regular Holiday | **Selected by the timekeeper** from a dropdown beside Date. No automated holiday calendar is maintained inside the system. |
| **Night-shift portion** | hours within the night-shift window                                                                                     | Computed from **Time In / Time Out** and the **night-shift window** (configuration).                                       |
| **Overtime portion**    | hours beyond regular working hours                                                                                      | Computed from **Time In / Time Out** against the regular working-hours rule (configuration).                               |

With those three facts captured per row, every "Work on" category in the DOLE matrix is a deterministic combination of (Day Type, Night-shift hours, Overtime hours): the system composes it, the timekeeper never picks from a forty-row dropdown, and the payroll manager never applies a multiplier by hand. The DOLE multiplier table itself becomes a **configuration table** owned by the future Accounting / Payroll module, applied automatically when payroll is run.

**JCE's multiplier composition rule (multiplicative — per DOLE).** _Confirmed in v1.39 from the DOLE Handbook on Workers' Statutory Monetary Benefits._ When an hour falls into more than one premium category (most commonly: an Overtime hour that is also inside the Night Differential window), JCE combines the premiums **multiplicatively**, matching the standard DOLE matrix. For example, an OT hour worked at 11:30 PM on a Regular Day is paid at **1.0 × 1.25 × 1.10 = 1.375 (137.5%)** of the base rate. A Rest Day worked at OT pays **1.3 × 1.3 = 1.69 (169%)**. A Regular Holiday on Sunday worked pays **2.0 × 1.3 = 2.6 (260%)**. The Settings sub-tab (Section 5.2.1) stores the precomputed multiplier matrix; the Payroll engine looks up the right cell for each hour worked.

> _Historical note:_ an earlier draft (v1.11) recorded the composition as additive based on an initial verbal confirmation. The DOLE Handbook source confirmed in v1.39 supersedes that — JCE follows the standard DOLE multiplicative composition. The numerical examples and the worked Regular-Holiday-on-Sunday case in this section have been updated accordingly.

**Data-model implication.** For payroll to apply the multiplicative compound multiplier (e.g., 137.5% for an Ordinary-day OT hour that is also in the night window), it needs to know **which of the OT hours are also Night Differential hours** — not just the counts independently. The Timekeeping data model still records Regular / Overtime / Night Differential as separate counts on each row (Section 4.2 "Manhours Distribution"), but at JCE the conventional pattern (per Bryan's worked example: 8 AM – 2 AM = 8 Regular + 8 OT + 3 Night Diff, where the 3 Night Diff hours are part of the 8 OT) makes the overlap deterministic: **Night Differential hours that exceed the 8-hour Regular threshold are by definition OT hours that also qualify for the night premium.** The Payroll engine derives this overlap from the row's worked window and Day Type at computation time, then applies the appropriate compound cell from the Settings matrix. Edge cases (e.g., a true night-shift worker whose Regular hours fall in the night window) require explicit per-hour categorization; these are flagged for review when the Payroll engine itself is detailed.

**Premium values reference (the row-level multipliers, JCE = DOLE).** _Confirmed._ The values below match the DOLE matrix stored in Section 5.2.1 Settings. Each row-level multiplier is expressed as a factor relative to the base daily/hourly rate.

| Multiplier                 | Value                                            | Applies when                                                                                                                                            |
| -------------------------- | ------------------------------------------------ | ------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| **Rest Day**               | × 1.3                                            | Row's Day Type = Rest Day; **or** Day Type is any Holiday type and the row's Date falls on a Sunday (auto-detected — multiplied with the holiday rate). |
| **Special Holiday**        | × 1.3                                            | Day Type = Special Holiday.                                                                                                                             |
| **Double Special Holiday** | × 1.5                                            | Day Type = Double Special Holiday.                                                                                                                      |
| **Regular Holiday**        | × 2.0                                            | Day Type = Regular Holiday.                                                                                                                             |
| **Double Regular Holiday** | × 3.0                                            | Day Type = Double Regular Holiday.                                                                                                                      |
| **Overtime**               | × 1.25 (ordinary day) or × 1.3 (other day types) | For each Overtime hour on the row.                                                                                                                      |
| **Night Differential**     | × 1.1                                            | For each Night Differential hour on the row.                                                                                                            |

Payroll math per hour, in plain terms: **base rate × (the row-level day-type multiplier) × (× 1.1 if Night Differential) × (× 1.25 or × 1.3 if Overtime)**. The factors **multiply**, not add. For the full DOLE compound matrix already-precomputed and stored in Settings, see Section 5.2.1 Section 1 (Pay Rate Multipliers).

**Compound case: holiday falling on Sunday.** _Confirmed (updated to multiplicative)._ When the timekeeper picks any holiday Day Type (Special Holiday, Double Special Holiday, Regular Holiday, or Double Regular Holiday) and the row's **Date is a Sunday**, the system applies the Holiday-on-Rest-Day cell of the DOLE matrix — equivalent to **Holiday multiplier × 1.3**. The system **auto-detects "is this Date a Sunday?" from the Date itself** using pure date arithmetic; no holiday calendar is needed, and the timekeeper still picks exactly one Day Type. Example values (matching the DOLE matrix in Section 5.2.1):

| Worked condition                 | Day Type picked        | Date is Sunday?                      | Multiplier applied | Total of base rate |
| -------------------------------- | ---------------------- | ------------------------------------ | ------------------ | ------------------ |
| Regular Holiday on a weekday     | Regular Holiday        | No                                   | × 2.0              | **200%**           |
| Regular Holiday on Sunday        | Regular Holiday        | Yes                                  | × 2.0 × 1.3        | **260%**           |
| Special Holiday on Sunday        | Special Holiday        | Yes                                  | × 1.3 × 1.3        | **169%**           |
| Double Regular Holiday on Sunday | Double Regular Holiday | Yes                                  | × 3.0 × 1.3        | **390%**           |
| Plain Sunday (no holiday)        | Rest Day               | (n/a — Day Type already captures it) | × 1.3              | **130%**           |

The Timekeeping data model needs **no change** to support this: the row still records one Day Type plus the usual Manhours Distribution counts; the Sunday-overlap is determined from the Date at payroll time. This logic — the auto-detect rule and the multiplicative composition — belongs in the **DOLE / payroll multiplier configuration** owned by the Accounting / Payroll module (Section 5.2.1).

**What each role does under this mapping.**

- **The timekeeper** enters: Date, Working Project, Day Type (from the dropdown), Time In, Time Out, the row's Manhours Distribution split, and any Remarks.
- **The payroll manager** sees finalized hours already tagged with their Day Type bucket. Payroll math is the multiplier table applied automatically; the manager reviews and approves, but does not reclassify hours.
- **The system** holds the DOLE multiplier table as a **lookup** — stored once, maintained centrally, and updated whenever DOLE revises rates. It is configuration, not per-row entry.

**Applied in this draft.** ✅ The Manhours Distribution above now reflects this model:

- **Six Day Type buckets**: Regular Day, **Rest Day** (= Sunday), Special Holiday, Double Special Holiday, Regular Holiday, Double Regular Holiday.
- **Day Type is selected by the timekeeper** from a dropdown beside Date — not auto-derived from a holiday calendar.
- **Rest Day is a Day Type value**, not a separate flag. It applies to Sundays for all employees.

**Derived requirements (implied by Manhours Distribution).** The pieces of configuration the system needs for the auto-calculate engine are listed under "Calculation rules" above (regular workday, lunch break, OT meal break, night meal break, night differential window). They are system-wide constants, not per-employee or per-date.

Two configuration items that were previously listed here — a **holiday calendar** and a **per-employee rest-day schedule** — are no longer needed. Day Type is selected by the timekeeper from a dropdown (no calendar to maintain), and Rest Day is fixed at Sunday for all employees (no per-employee schedule).

**Pay periods, verification, and hand-off to payroll.** Timekeeping data is **verified by the timekeeper** at the end of each pay period, then **locked** and handed off to the Accounting / Payroll module for payroll processing.

_Pay period types._ Different employees are paid on different schedules at JCE. Each employee's schedule is determined by their **Salary Rate Category** (a field from the PIS — see Section 4.1). Three schedules are supported:

| Type        | Cutoff schedule                                                       | Pay frequency                            |
| ----------- | --------------------------------------------------------------------- | ---------------------------------------- |
| **Monthly** | **8–22** and **23–7** (the second period crosses into the next month) | Semi-monthly (two pay periods per month) |
| **Daily**   | **6–20** and **21–5** (the second period crosses into the next month) | Semi-monthly (two pay periods per month) |
| **Weekly**  | **Sunday – Saturday**                                                 | Weekly (one pay period per week)         |

> Naming note: "Monthly" and "Daily" refer to the employee's **rate basis** (how their pay is expressed), not the payment frequency. Both rate bases are actually paid **semi-monthly** — twice per month, on the cutoff ranges above. Only "Weekly" employees are paid once per period.

_(To confirm: that **Salary Rate Category** is indeed the field on the employee record that drives pay-period type — if JCE uses a separate field for this, point me to it.)_

_Data entry rhythm vs. pay period._ Time is entered **week by week** (one Sunday–Saturday block at a time), regardless of an employee's pay period type, matching the existing spreadsheet rhythm. The system then aggregates each week's entries into the right pay period for that employee:

- For **Monthly** and **Daily** employees, two weeks' entries (give or take, depending on calendar alignment) roll up into one semi-monthly pay period.
- For **Weekly** employees, one week's entries _is_ one pay period.

_Verification workflow._ Once an employee's pay period closes (their applicable cutoff date passes), the system bundles all their timekeeping rows for that period into a **verification batch**. The batch can contain both rows the timekeeper entered manually and rows the system auto-created from approved HR Requests (currently: Leave of Absence forms — see Section 4.3.5). The timekeeper:

1. **Reviews** the batch — every row, every Day Type, every Manhours Distribution number, including any rows auto-created from Leave records (marked accordingly so they're distinguishable).
2. **Corrects** any errors (re-opening a row to edit Date, Working Project, Day Type, Time In, or Time Out; the auto-calculate engine recomputes the Manhours Distribution on save).
3. **Marks the batch as Verified.**

Once verified, the batch is **locked** — no further edits are allowed without an explicit re-open action. The verified batch is the unit of hand-off to the future Accounting / Payroll module: one employee, one pay period, every row tagged with its Day Type, Working Project, and Manhours Distribution counts, ready for the multiplicative DOLE pay-rate math (Section 5.2.1).

_(To confirm: whether re-opening a verified batch should require a specific role/permission beyond ordinary HR access, and whether the system should log who verified each batch and when, for HR/owner visibility. Proposed default: re-open is allowed for the timekeeper with reason captured in an audit trail; verification timestamp and verifier are recorded on each batch.)_

**Source documents.**

> **Status: Spreadsheet structure received and incorporated.** ✅ The field list and Manhours Distribution buckets above come from the company's existing timekeeping spreadsheet. What remains is a **sample of the actual spreadsheet** — for the exact on-screen layout (column order, grouping, weekly view), totals/formulas, and any cell-level rules — so the entry screen can mirror it faithfully.

**Notes for design.** The weekly, per-employee, per-project structure, the entry field list, and the Manhours Distribution buckets are enough to start shaping the data model and the entry screen. The holiday calendar, per-employee rest day, and night differential window should be modeled as **configuration data** the system maintains, so the distribution can be classified consistently across employees and weeks.

### 4.3 HR Requests

**Purpose.** To digitally **record** HR-related requests at JCE so the company has a structured, searchable database of requests, with each record linked to the relevant employee(s), project, and (where applicable) timekeeping or leave balance.

**Workflow model — paper-first, system records.** _Confirmed for all five HR request forms._ Requests at JCE are filled out by the employee on **paper**. The paper form is signed sequentially by the actual approvers (the exact signature chain depends on the form type — for OB/Travel, it is Approving Officer / Dept. Head → Admin and Finance → HR Acknowledges; the approval chains for the other four forms will be specified in their respective subsections once scoped). Once the paper form is fully signed, **HR enters the form's data into the system and uploads a scanned copy** of the signed hard copy. The system is therefore a **digital record-keeping layer** — the paper form is the source of truth and the audit; the system makes the data structured, searchable, and connected to other modules. The system does _not_ implement a digital approval workflow for any HR request type; signatures happen on paper.

**Confirmed scope.** Five request types, each based on an existing company form:

1. **Official Business / Travel Form** — _fully scoped in Section 4.3.1._
2. **Overtime (OT) Form** — _fully scoped in Section 4.3.2._
3. **Request for Leave** — _fully scoped in Section 4.3.3._ This is the form JCE uses for all paid leave requests; per Bryan, this _is_ what was originally listed as "Leave with Pay."
4. ~~Leave of Absence Form — With Pay~~ — **consolidated with Request for Leave (#3 above).** The original list of five forms is in practice four: the Request for Leave form covers what was being called "Leave with Pay."
5. **Leave of Absence Form — Without Pay** — _fully scoped in Section 4.3.5._

> **HR Requests is now fully scoped** — all four distinct forms (4.3.1, 4.3.2, 4.3.3, 4.3.5) have been mapped out. The Section 4.3.4 number is intentionally skipped due to the consolidation noted above.

**Leave balances — not tracked.** _Confirmed._ JCE does **not** maintain leave entitlements per employee. There is no annual allotment for Vacation, Sick, Paternity, or Maternity leave that the system needs to enforce. Leave-type requests (Request for Leave and LOA Without Pay) are **recorded only**, for audit and HR reporting purposes.
